fields.name: 03. Work in the Constitution and Related Issues
fields.content-01:
1. Introduction: Work and Human Rights (10 minutes)

‍

Objective: Introduce labour rights as human rights and activate prior knowledge.

Teacher input:
The teacher introduces the idea that labour rights are part of fundamental human rights and briefly presents key principles from international human rights charters related to work.

Key labour rights from international human rights charters include:

  • The right to work: Everyone has the right to work and to freely choose their employment.
  • The right to just and favourable working conditions: This includes fair wages, equal pay for equal work, safe and healthy working conditions, and reasonable working hours.
  • The right to protection against unemployment: States should take measures to reduce unemployment and provide support when work is not available.
  • The right to form and join trade unions: Workers have the right to organise and defend their interests collectively.
  • The right to rest and leisure: This includes limited working hours and paid holidays.
  • Protection from exploitation and forced labour: No one should be forced to work under coercion or degrading conditions.

Activity:

  • In pairs, students discuss:
    • Which of these rights they find most important.
    • Which rights they think are most difficult to guarantee in practice.
  • A short whole-class discussion follows.
    ‍

2. Research Phase: National Laws and Labour Rights (20 minutes)

‍

Objective: Connect international labour rights with national legal frameworks.

Activity:

  • Students work in small groups.
  • Each group researches:
    • How labour and workers’ rights are protected in their country’s constitution.
    • Which laws or regulations protect workers (e.g. minimum wage, working hours, safety, contracts, unions).
  • Groups take notes on:
    • Rights that are clearly protected
    • Rights that are weakly protected or controversial

‍

3. Introduction to Motions and Motion Analysis (10 minutes)

‍

Teacher input:

  • The teacher explains what a motion is and introduces motion analysis:
    • Identifying key terms
    • Clarifying what the motion is about
    • Understanding who is affected
    • Identifying possible clashes
      ‍

4. Group Work: Motion Analysis and Brainstorming (30 minutes)

‍

Objective: Analyse labour-related issues and develop structured ideas.

Activity:

  • Students are divided into groups.
  • Each group receives one motion (see list below).
  • Tasks:
    1. Conduct a motion analysis.
    2. Brainstorm arguments for and against the motion.
    3. Connect arguments to:
      • International labour rights
      • National laws and real-life examples
        ‍

5. Group Presentations (15 minutes)

‍

Objective: Share findings and practice public speaking.

Activity:

  • Each group gives a short presentation (3–4 minutes) including:
    • Their motion analysis
    • Key arguments for and against
    • Links to human rights and national legislation
  • Short audience questions after each presentation.
    ‍

6. Conclusion and Reflection (5 minutes)
‍

Discussion questions:

  • Which labour rights seem most important today?
  • Where do you see the biggest gap between law and reality?
  • How can young people protect their labour rights?

Motions on Labour and Workers’ Rights

  1. This house believes that the right to work should be guaranteed by the state.
  2. This house believes that minimum wage laws should be the same for all workers.
  3. This house believes that job security is more important than labour market flexibility.
  4. This house believes that unpaid internships exploit young workers.
  5. This house believes that trade unions are essential for protecting workers’ rights.
  6. This house believes that automation threatens fundamental labour rights.
  7. This house believes that governments should strictly limit working hours.
  8. This house believes that migrant workers’ rights are not sufficiently protected.
  9. This house believes that companies should be legally responsible for workers’ mental health.
  10. This house believes that economic growth should never come at the cost of workers’ rights.
fields.content-02:
Objectives

‍

Subject-specific goals:

  • Students will understand key labour rights as defined in international human rights documents.
  • Students will analyse how labour rights are protected through national constitutions and labour laws in their own countries.
  • Students will identify similarities and differences between international standards and national legislation.
  • Students will explore contemporary issues related to work, employment, and workers’ rights.

Cross-disciplinary goals:

  • Students will develop critical thinking and research skills.
  • Students will practice motion analysis and structured brainstorming.
  • Students will improve presentation and public speaking skills.
  • Students will collaborate effectively in small groups.
fields.content-03:
Materials / Tools

‍

  • Extracts from international human rights documents related to work (e.g. right to work, fair pay, safe working conditions, freedom of association)
  • Access to national constitutions and labour laws (online or printed)
  • Flipchart paper or posters and markers
  • Motion analysis worksheet
  • Timer
fields.content-05:
Assessment

‍

Formative assessment:

  • Participation in research and group work
  • Quality of motion analysis and brainstorming

Summative assessment:

  • Group presentation (clarity, structure, use of evidence, teamwork)

‍

Tips for Teachers

‍

  • Allow students to use examples from their own countries to increase relevance.
  • Support lower-level students with sentence starters for motion analysis.
  • Encourage respectful discussion, especially when national laws differ.

‍
